input.content:
name: Hardware Timing Validation

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'packages/timing/**'
      - 'packages/forgescript/**'
      - '.github/workflows/timing-validation.yml'
  schedule:
    # Run daily to catch browser/OS changes that might affect timing
    - cron: '0 0 * * *'
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      duration:
        description: 'Test duration in milliseconds'
        required: false
        default: '3000'
      targetFps:
        description: 'Target frame rate for testing'
        required: false
        default: '60'

jobs:
  validate-timing:
    name: Validate Timing on ${{ matrix.os }}
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest]
        node-version: [18.x, 20.x]
        browser: [chromium, firefox, webkit]
        exclude:
          # WebKit not well supported on Windows
          - os: windows-latest
            browser: webkit
      fail-fast: false

    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
          cache: 'npm'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Build packages
        run: npm run build

      - name: Install Playwright browsers
        run: npx playwright install ${{ matrix.browser }} --with-deps

      - name: Run timing validation - ${{ matrix.browser }}
        id: timing-validation
        run: |
          node packages/timing/scripts/ci-timing-validation-playwright.js \
            --browser ${{ matrix.browser }} \
            --duration ${{ github.event.inputs.duration || '3000' }} \
            --fps ${{ github.event.inputs.targetFps || '60' }} \
            --output timing-results-${{ matrix.os }}-${{ matrix.browser }}.json \
            --fail-on-5ms
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Upload timing results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: timing-results-${{ matrix.os }}-${{ matrix.node-version }}-${{ matrix.browser }}
          path: timing-results-*.json

      - name: Parse timing results
        if: always()
        id: parse-results
        run: |
          if [ -f "timing-results-${{ matrix.os }}-${{ matrix.browser }}.json" ]; then
            # Extract key metrics using jq or similar
            echo "Results found for ${{ matrix.os }} - ${{ matrix.browser }}"
            cat timing-results-${{ matrix.os }}-${{ matrix.browser }}.json | \
              node -e "
                const data = JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync(0, 'utf-8'));
                const r = data.results;
                console.log('Platform:', r.deviceCapabilities.platform);
                console.log('2ms Precision:', r.compatibilityMatrix.canRun2msPrecision ? '✅' : '❌');
                console.log('5ms Precision:', r.compatibilityMatrix.canRun5msPrecision ? '✅' : '❌');
                console.log('FPS:', r.fpsMetrics.actualFps.toFixed(1));
                console.log('Clock Resolution:', r.timingPrecision.clockResolution.toFixed(0) + 'μs');
              "
          fi
        shell: bash

      - name: Check timing requirements
        if: steps.timing-validation.outcome == 'failure'
        run: |
          echo "::error::Timing validation failed on ${{ matrix.os }} with ${{ matrix.browser }}"
          exit 1

  timing-report:
    name: Generate Timing Report
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: validate-timing
    if: always()
    
    steps:
      - name: Download all artifacts
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          path: timing-results

      - name: Generate consolidated report
        run: |
          echo "# Hardware Timing Validation Report" > timing-report.md
          echo "" >> timing-report.md
          echo "Generated: $(date -u +'%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S UTC')" >> timing-report.md
          echo "" >> timing-report.md
          
          echo "## Summary" >> timing-report.md
          echo "" >> timing-report.md
          
          # Process all result files
          for file in timing-results/*/timing-results-*.json; do
            if [ -f "$file" ]; then
              echo "### $(basename $file .json)" >> timing-report.md
              
              node -e "
                const data = JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('$file', 'utf-8'));
                const r = data.results;
                console.log('- Platform:', r.deviceCapabilities.platform);
                console.log('- WebGL:', r.deviceCapabilities.webGL2Supported ? 'WebGL2' : 
                           r.deviceCapabilities.webGL1Supported ? 'WebGL1' : 'None');
                console.log('- 2ms Precision:', r.compatibilityMatrix.canRun2msPrecision ? '✅ Supported' : '❌ Not Supported');
                console.log('- 5ms Precision:', r.compatibilityMatrix.canRun5msPrecision ? '✅ Supported' : '❌ Not Supported');
                console.log('- Actual FPS:', r.fpsMetrics.actualFps.toFixed(1));
                console.log('- Clock Resolution:', r.timingPrecision.clockResolution.toFixed(0) + 'μs');
                console.log('- Timing Jitter:', r.timingPrecision.jitter.toFixed(2) + 'ms');
                console.log('- Dropped Frames:', r.fpsMetrics.droppedFrames);
                if (r.compatibilityMatrix.warnings.length > 0) {
                  console.log('- Warnings:');
                  r.compatibilityMatrix.warnings.forEach(w => console.log('  - ' + w));
                }
                console.log('');
              " >> timing-report.md
            fi
          done

      - name: Upload timing report
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: timing-validation-report
          path: timing-report.md

      - name: Comment PR with results
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const report = fs.readFileSync('timing-report.md', 'utf8');
            
            // Find existing comment
            const { data: comments } = await github.rest.issues.listComments({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
            });
            
            const botComment = comments.find(comment => 
              comment.user.type === 'Bot' && 
              comment.body.includes('Hardware Timing Validation Report')
            );
            
            const body = `## 🔬 Hardware Timing Validation Results\n\n${report}`;
            
            if (botComment) {
              await github.rest.issues.updateComment({
                owner: context.repo.owner,
                repo: context.repo.repo,
                comment_id: botComment.id,
                body
              });
            } else {
              await github.rest.issues.createComment({
                owner: context.repo.owner,
                repo: context.repo.repo,
                issue_number: context.issue.number,
                body
              });
            }

  performance-regression:
    name: Check Performance Regression
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: validate-timing
    if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Download current results
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          path: current-results

      - name: Download previous results
        uses: dawidd6/action-download-artifact@v3
        with:
          workflow: timing-validation.yml
          branch: main
          name: timing-results-ubuntu-latest-20.x-chromium
          path: previous-results
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Compare results
        run: |
          if [ -d "previous-results" ]; then
            echo "Comparing with previous results..."
            # Add comparison logic here
            # Could check for degradation in timing precision, FPS, etc.
          else
            echo "No previous results found for comparison"
          fi

      - name: Store benchmark results
        uses: benchmark-action/github-action-benchmark@v1
        with:
          tool: 'customSmallerIsBetter'
          output-file-path: current-results/timing-results-ubuntu-latest-chromium.json
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          auto-push: true

input.content:
# Hardware Timing Validation Suite

The Hardware Timing Validation Suite ensures that Psycho-Forge can deliver on its promise of <2ms stimulus timing precision across different hardware configurations.

## Overview

The validation suite tests:
- Frame rate stability (60Hz, 120Hz, 144Hz displays)
- Clock resolution and precision
- WebGL compatibility and performance
- Memory usage patterns
- Timing jitter and drift
- Browser-specific timing capabilities

## Components

### 1. HardwareTimingValidator Class

Located in `src/HardwareTimingValidator.ts`, this class performs comprehensive timing tests:

```typescript
import { HardwareTimingValidator } from '@psyforge/timing';

const validator = new HardwareTimingValidator();
const results = await validator.validate({
  testDuration: 3000,  // 3 seconds
  targetFps: 60        // Target frame rate
});
```

### 2. Browser Runner

The HTML runner (`src/browser-runner/timing-validation-runner.html`) provides:
- Visual interface for manual testing
- Automated testing via URL parameters
- CI integration support

Access via: `http://localhost:3000/timing-validation-runner.html?autorun=true`

### 3. CI Integration Scripts

#### Playwright Script (Recommended)
```bash
node scripts/ci-timing-validation-playwright.js \
  --browser chromium \
  --duration 5000 \
  --fail-on-5ms \
  --output results.json
```

Supports multiple browsers:
- `chromium` - Chrome/Edge
- `firefox` - Firefox
- `webkit` - Safari

#### Puppeteer Script (Alternative)
```bash
node scripts/ci-timing-validation.js \
  --duration 5000 \
  --fail-on-5ms \
  --output results.json
```

## Running Validation

### Local Development

```bash
# Run validation for all browsers
npm run validate:timing:all

# Run for specific browser
npm run validate:timing:chromium
npm run validate:timing:firefox
npm run validate:timing:webkit

# Run with CI failure conditions
npm run validate:timing:ci
```

### Manual Testing

1. Build the project: `npm run build`
2. Serve the HTML runner
3. Open in browser: `http://localhost:3000/timing-validation-runner.html`
4. Click "Run Validation"

### CI/CD Pipeline

The GitHub Actions workflow (`/.github/workflows/timing-validation.yml`) runs automatically on:
- Push to main/develop branches
- Pull requests affecting timing code
- Daily schedule (catches browser updates)
- Manual dispatch

## Validation Results

### Key Metrics

1. **Device Capabilities**
   - WebGL support (WebGL2/WebGL1/None)
   - Hardware concurrency (CPU cores)
   - Performance.now() resolution
   - Display refresh rate

2. **Frame Rate Performance**
   - Actual vs target FPS
   - Frame time variance
   - Dropped frame count
   - FPS stability

3. **Timing Precision**
   - Clock resolution (microseconds)
   - Timing jitter (milliseconds)
   - Drift rate (percentage)
   - Maximum deviation

4. **Compatibility Matrix**
   - 2ms precision capability
   - 5ms precision capability
   - 16ms precision capability (always supported)
   - Recommended frame rate

### Precision Requirements

#### 2ms Precision (<2ms timing accuracy)
Requires ALL of:
- Audio Context support
- WebGL2 support
- Clock resolution ≤ 100μs
- Timing jitter < 0.5ms
- Frame time variance < 1ms

#### 5ms Precision (<5ms timing accuracy)
Requires:
- Audio Context support
- WebGL1 or WebGL2 support
- Clock resolution ≤ 1000μs
- Timing jitter < 2ms

#### 16ms Precision (frame-level accuracy)
- Always supported with requestAnimationFrame
- Suitable for non-critical timing tasks

## CI Requirements

The CI pipeline enforces:
- 5ms precision must be supported (default)
- Less than 10 dropped frames
- Timing jitter < 5ms
- Frame time variance < 5ms

Optional stricter requirements:
- 2ms precision support (`--fail-on-2ms`)
- Custom thresholds via workflow inputs

## Interpreting Results

### Success Indicators
```
✅ 2ms Precision: Supported
✅ 5ms Precision: Supported
✅ Actual FPS: 59.9 (target: 60)
✅ Clock Resolution: 45μs
✅ Timing Jitter: 0.3ms
```

### Warning Conditions
```
⚠️ High frame time variance (3.2ms) - timing may be unstable
⚠️ No WebGL support detected - performance will be limited
⚠️ High timing jitter (2.1ms) - precision degraded
```

### Failure Conditions
```
❌ 2ms Precision: Not Supported
❌ CI FAILED: 5ms precision requirement not met
❌ High dropped frame count (15)
```

## Performance Optimization

### For Better Results

1. **Close unnecessary applications** - Reduces CPU/GPU contention
2. **Use dedicated graphics** - Better frame rate stability
3. **Disable power saving** - Prevents CPU throttling
4. **Update browser** - Latest versions have better timing APIs
5. **Use appropriate display** - Higher refresh rates improve precision

### Hardware Recommendations

For <2ms precision experiments:
- Modern CPU (2020+)
- Dedicated GPU
- 8GB+ RAM
- 120Hz+ display
- Chrome/Edge browser

## Troubleshooting

### Common Issues

1. **"No WebGL support detected"**
   - Update graphics drivers
   - Enable hardware acceleration in browser
   - Check browser compatibility

2. **"High timing jitter"**
   - Close background applications
   - Disable browser extensions
   - Check for thermal throttling

3. **"Low clock resolution"**
   - Browser limitation on some platforms
   - Try different browser
   - Check OS timer resolution settings

4. **CI Failures**
   - Review specific metrics in logs
   - Check browser version changes
   - Verify no regression in code

## Future Enhancements

1. **Hardware blacklist/whitelist** - Known good/bad configurations
2. **Real device testing** - Physical timing measurement integration
3. **Network latency compensation** - For online experiments
4. **Mobile device support** - Touch timing validation
5. **VR/AR timing** - Extended reality support

## References

- [High Resolution Time API](https://www.w3.org/TR/hr-time-3/)
- [Web Audio API](https://www.w3.org/TR/webaudio/)
- [requestAnimationFrame timing](https://www.w3.org/TR/animation-timing/)
- [Browser Frame Timing](https://wicg.github.io/frame-timing/)

# ForgeScript API Request for Comments (RFC) Process

## Overview

The ForgeScript API RFC process ensures that new API additions are carefully considered, maintain consistency, and don't contribute to API sprawl. All significant API changes must go through this process.

## When is an RFC Required?

An RFC is required for:
- New top-level API methods
- New builder methods on core types
- Breaking changes to existing APIs
- New experiment patterns or templates
- Significant changes to API behavior

An RFC is NOT required for:
- Bug fixes that don't change API behavior
- Internal implementation changes
- Documentation improvements
- Adding TypeScript types without runtime changes

## RFC Template

```markdown
# RFC-XXXX: [Title]

**Status:** Draft | Under Review | Accepted | Rejected | Implemented
**Author:** [Your Name]
**Created:** [Date]
**Updated:** [Date]

## Summary

Brief description of the proposed API change (2-3 sentences).

## Motivation

Why is this change needed? What problem does it solve?

## Detailed Design

### API Surface

```typescript
// Show the exact API being proposed
interface ProposedAPI {
  // ...
}
```

### Usage Examples

```typescript
// Show how users would use this API
const example = experiment()
  .newFeature()
  .build();
```

### Implementation Strategy

How will this be implemented without breaking existing code?

## Alternatives Considered

What other approaches were considered and why were they rejected?

## Migration Path

If this deprecates existing APIs, how will users migrate?

## Impact Analysis

- **Complexity:** How much does this add to API surface area?
- **Learning Curve:** How easy is it for new users to understand?
- **Maintenance:** What's the long-term maintenance burden?
- **Performance:** Any performance implications?

## Open Questions

List any unresolved questions or concerns.
```

## Review Process

1. **Draft Phase** (1 week)
   - Author creates RFC following template
   - Initial feedback from immediate team
   - Refine proposal based on feedback

2. **Public Review** (2 weeks)
   - RFC posted to GitHub discussions
   - Community feedback collected
   - Author responds to questions/concerns

3. **Final Review** (1 week)
   - Core team makes decision
   - RFC marked as Accepted/Rejected
   - If accepted, implementation can begin

4. **Implementation** (varies)
   - PR created referencing RFC
   - Code review ensures RFC compliance
   - Documentation updated

## Decision Criteria

RFCs are evaluated based on:

1. **Necessity** - Does this solve a real problem?
2. **Simplicity** - Is this the simplest solution?
3. **Consistency** - Does it fit with existing APIs?
4. **Composability** - Does it work well with other features?
5. **Future-proof** - Will this still make sense in 2 years?

## API Design Principles

All RFCs should follow these principles:

1. **Progressive Disclosure** - Simple things should be simple
2. **Consistency** - Similar things should work similarly
3. **Composability** - Features should combine naturally
4. **Type Safety** - APIs should be fully typed
5. **Performance** - APIs shouldn't encourage inefficient patterns
6. **Backward Compatibility** - Avoid breaking changes when possible

## Examples of Good RFCs

### RFC-0001: Keyboard Response Presets

**Problem:** Users repeatedly define common keyboard mappings
**Solution:** Add preset configurations for common patterns
**API:** `response.keyboard.preset('likert-5')`
**Impact:** Low - additive change, reduces boilerplate

### RFC-0002: Timeline Templates

**Problem:** Common paradigms require lots of boilerplate
**Solution:** Add paradigm-specific timeline templates
**API:** `timeline.template('n-back', { n: 2 })`
**Impact:** Medium - new concept but high value

## Examples of RFCs to Avoid

```typescript
// BAD: Too many options in one method
experiment()

    display: {...},
    responses: {...},
    data: {...},

  })
```

```

// BAD: Inconsistent naming patterns

response.insertKey()
```

- APIs marked stable cannot be removed without deprecation

- Deprecated APIs must have migration guides

# ForgeScript API Request for Comments (RFC) Process

## Overview

The ForgeScript API RFC process ensures that new API additions are carefully considered, maintain consistency, and don't contribute to API sprawl. All significant API changes must go through this process.

## When is an RFC Required?

An RFC is required for:
- New top-level API methods
- New builder methods on core types
- Breaking changes to existing APIs
- New experiment patterns or templates
- Significant changes to API behavior

An RFC is NOT required for:
- Bug fixes that don't change API behavior
- Internal implementation changes
- Documentation improvements
- Adding TypeScript types without runtime changes

## RFC Template

```markdown
# RFC-XXXX: [Title]

**Status:** Draft | Under Review | Accepted | Rejected | Implemented
**Author:** [Your Name]
**Created:** [Date]
**Updated:** [Date]

## Summary

Brief description of the proposed API change (2-3 sentences).

## Motivation

Why is this change needed? What problem does it solve?

## Detailed Design

### API Surface

```typescript
// Show the exact API being proposed
interface ProposedAPI {
  // ...
}
```

### Usage Examples

```typescript
// Show how users would use this API
const example = experiment()
  .newFeature()
  .build();
```

### Implementation Strategy

How will this be implemented without breaking existing code?

## Alternatives Considered

What other approaches were considered and why were they rejected?

## Migration Path

If this deprecates existing APIs, how will users migrate?

## Impact Analysis

- **Complexity:** How much does this add to API surface area?
- **Learning Curve:** How easy is it for new users to understand?
- **Maintenance:** What's the long-term maintenance burden?
- **Performance:** Any performance implications?

## Open Questions

List any unresolved questions or concerns.
```

## Review Process

1. **Draft Phase** (1 week)
   - Author creates RFC following template
   - Initial feedback from immediate team
   - Refine proposal based on feedback

2. **Public Review** (2 weeks)
   - RFC posted to GitHub discussions
   - Community feedback collected
   - Author responds to questions/concerns

3. **Final Review** (1 week)
   - Core team makes decision
   - RFC marked as Accepted/Rejected
   - If accepted, implementation can begin

4. **Implementation** (varies)
   - PR created referencing RFC
   - Code review ensures RFC compliance
   - Documentation updated

## Decision Criteria

RFCs are evaluated based on:

1. **Necessity** - Does this solve a real problem?
2. **Simplicity** - Is this the simplest solution?
3. **Consistency** - Does it fit with existing APIs?
4. **Composability** - Does it work well with other features?
5. **Future-proof** - Will this still make sense in 2 years?

# ForgeScript API Deprecation Policy

## Overview

This policy ensures that ForgeScript maintains backward compatibility while allowing the API to evolve. All deprecations must follow this process to minimize disruption to users.

## Deprecation Timeline

### Phase 1: Deprecation Notice (Version X.Y)
- Mark API as `@deprecated` with JSDoc
- Add console warning when deprecated API is used
- Provide migration guide in documentation
- Announce in release notes

### Phase 2: Soft Deprecation (Version X+1.0)
- Deprecated API still works but warnings are more prominent
- IDE/linter warnings become errors
- Migration tools provided if applicable

### Phase 3: Hard Deprecation (Version X+2.0)
- API removed from TypeScript types
- Runtime still works but throws deprecation error
- Clear error message with migration path

### Phase 4: Removal (Version X+3.0)
- API completely removed
- Use results in runtime error

## Deprecation Guidelines

### What Can Be Deprecated

1. **Redundant APIs** - Multiple ways to do the same thing
2. **Problematic APIs** - Design flaws that can't be fixed
3. **Obsolete APIs** - No longer relevant or superseded
4. **Performance Issues** - APIs that encourage inefficient patterns

### What Cannot Be Deprecated

1. **Core APIs** used by >50% of experiments
2. **APIs less than 2 major versions old**
3. **APIs without a migration path**
4. **APIs required for backward compatibility**

## Deprecation Annotations

### TypeScript/JSDoc Format

```typescript
/**
 * Creates a text stimulus
 * @deprecated Since version 2.0. Use `stimulus.text()` instead.
 * @see {@link https://docs.psyforge.org/migration/v2#createTextStimulus}
 * 
 * @example
 * // Old way (deprecated)
 * const text = createTextStimulus('Hello');
 * 
 * // New way
 * const text = stimulus.text('Hello');
 */
export function createTextStimulus(content: string): TextStimulus {
  console.warn(
    'createTextStimulus is deprecated and will be removed in v4.0. ' +
    'Use stimulus.text() instead. See migration guide: ' +
    'https://docs.psyforge.org/migration/v2#createTextStimulus'
  );
  return new TextStimulus(crypto.randomUUID(), content);
}
```

### Runtime Warnings

```typescript
class DeprecationWarning {
  private static warnings = new Set<string>();
  
  static warn(api: string, alternative: string, removeVersion: string) {
    const key = `${api}-${alternative}`;
    
    // Only warn once per session
    if (this.warnings.has(key)) return;
    this.warnings.add(key);
    
    const message = `DEPRECATION WARNING: ${api} is deprecated and will be removed in ${removeVersion}. Use ${alternative} instead.`;
    
    if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development') {
      console.warn(message);
      console.trace(); // Show stack trace in development
    } else {
      console.warn(message);
    }
  }
}
```

## Migration Tools

### Automated Migration

For major deprecations, provide automated migration tools:

```bash
# Migrate from v1 to v2
npx @psyforge/migrate --from 1.0 --to 2.0 ./src

# Dry run to see what would change
npx @psyforge/migrate --from 1.0 --to 2.0 --dry-run ./src
```

### Migration Guides

Each deprecated API must have a migration guide with:

1. **Why** the API was deprecated
2. **What** to use instead
3. **How** to migrate (with examples)
4. **When** it will be removed

Example migration guide:

```markdown
## Migrating from createTextStimulus

The `createTextStimulus` function has been deprecated in favor of the new builder API.

### Why?
- Reduces API surface area
- More consistent with other stimulus types
- Better TypeScript inference

### Before
```typescript
const text = createTextStimulus('Hello World');
text.duration = 1000;
text.color = '#FF0000';
```

### After
```typescript
const text = stimulus.text('Hello World')
  .duration(1000)
  .color('#FF0000');
```

### Automated Migration
Run: `npx @psyforge/migrate fix-create-stimulus`
```

## Experimental APIs

### Marking Experimental

APIs can be marked as experimental to indicate they may change:

```typescript
/**
 * @experimental This API is experimental and may change without notice.
 * @since 2.1
 */
export function experimentalFeature() {
  // ...
}
```

### Experimental API Rules

1. Can change without deprecation notice
2. Must be clearly marked in documentation
3. Should emit warning when used
4. Cannot be used in stable APIs

## Version Support

### LTS (Long Term Support)

- Every 3rd major version is LTS
- LTS versions supported for 2 years
- Only critical fixes backported

### Version Matrix

| Version | Status | Support Until | Notes |
|---------|--------|---------------|-------|
| 1.0 LTS | Stable | Dec 2025 | Security fixes only |
| 2.0 | Stable | Jun 2024 | Bug fixes |
| 3.0 LTS | Stable | Dec 2026 | Current LTS |
| 4.0 | Beta | - | Next major |

## Communication

### Deprecation Announcements

1. **Release Notes** - List all deprecations
2. **Blog Post** - For major deprecations
3. **Email** - For breaking changes (opt-in)
4. **IDE Warnings** - Via TypeScript declarations
5. **Runtime Warnings** - Console messages

### Feedback Channels

- GitHub Discussions for deprecation feedback
- RFC process for controversial deprecations
- Survey users before major removals

## Emergency Deprecation

In rare cases (security, critical bugs), faster deprecation allowed:

1. **Security Issue** - Can be removed in next patch
2. **Data Loss Bug** - Can be removed in next minor
3. **Legal Issue** - Immediate removal allowed

Must still provide migration path where possible.

## Metrics and Review

Track deprecation impact:

1. **Usage Metrics** - How many use deprecated APIs
2. **Migration Rate** - How fast users migrate
3. **Support Burden** - Issues related to deprecated APIs
4. **User Feedback** - Satisfaction with deprecation process

Review policy annually and adjust based on metrics.

# Migration Guide: ForgeScript vX.Y to vX.Z

## Overview

This guide helps you migrate your ForgeScript experiments from version X.Y to X.Z. 

**Estimated migration time:** 
- Simple experiments: 5-10 minutes
- Complex experiments: 30-60 minutes
- With automated tools: 5 minutes

## Breaking Changes

### 1. [Change Name]

**What changed:** Brief description of what changed

**Why:** Rationale for the change

**Before:**
```typescript
// Old code example
experiment.oldMethod();
```

**After:**
```typescript
// New code example
experiment.newMethod();
```

**Automated migration:**
```bash
npx @psyforge/migrate fix-old-method
```

### 2. [Another Change]

[Follow same pattern]

## Deprecated APIs

The following APIs are deprecated and will be removed in version X+2:

| Deprecated API | Replacement | Remove Version |
|----------------|-------------|----------------|
| `createStimulus()` | `stimulus.create()` | 4.0 |
| `Timeline.addStimulus()` | `Timeline.add()` | 4.0 |

## New Features

While migrating, consider using these new features:

### Feature 1: [Name]
```typescript
// Example of new feature
experiment.newFeature()
  .withOption('value')
  .build();
```

## Migration Checklist

- [ ] Update ForgeScript to latest version
- [ ] Run automated migration tool
- [ ] Fix any remaining TypeScript errors
- [ ] Update deprecated API usage
- [ ] Test experiments thoroughly
- [ ] Update documentation

## Common Issues

### Issue 1: TypeScript errors after update

**Problem:** Getting TypeScript errors about missing properties

**Solution:** 
```typescript
// Add missing required properties
const config = {
  oldProp: value,
  newRequiredProp: defaultValue // Add this
};
```

### Issue 2: Runtime warnings

**Problem:** Console warnings about deprecated APIs

**Solution:** Follow the deprecation warnings to update your code

## Getting Help

- 📖 [Full API Documentation](https://docs.psyforge.org)
- 💬 [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/psyforge/discussions)
- 🐛 [Report Issues](https://github.com/psyforge/issues)

## Rollback Instructions

If you need to rollback:

1. Downgrade ForgeScript: `npm install @psyforge/forgescript@X.Y`
2. Revert code changes: `git revert [migration-commit]`
3. Clear node_modules: `rm -rf node_modules && npm install`

Note: We recommend fixing forward rather than rolling back.

input.content:
# API Complexity Management

ForgeScript uses a comprehensive system to prevent API sprawl and maintain a clean, learnable interface.

## Overview

The API complexity management system includes:
- RFC process for new API additions
- Automated API surface linting
- JSON declarative format for simple use cases
- Deprecation policy with migration tools
- Complexity metrics and reporting

## RFC Process

All significant API changes require an RFC (Request for Comments):

1. **Create RFC** following the template in `src/api/governance/RFC.md`
2. **Submit for review** via GitHub PR
3. **Community feedback** period (2 weeks)
4. **Core team decision** based on criteria
5. **Implementation** if approved

### Example RFC

```markdown
# RFC-0042: Add Paradigm Templates

**Status:** Under Review
**Author:** Jane Developer
**Created:** 2024-01-15

## Summary
Add pre-built templates for common experimental paradigms (Stroop, N-back, etc.)

## Motivation
Researchers repeatedly implement the same paradigms from scratch...
```

## API Linting

Run the API linter to check complexity:

```bash
# Check API complexity
npm run api:check

# Generate detailed report
npm run api:report

# CI integration
npm run api:lint -- --fail-on-warnings
```

### Complexity Metrics

The linter tracks:
- Total exports
- Public methods
- Public classes
- Builder methods
- Deprecated APIs
- Overall complexity score

### Thresholds

| Metric | Target | Maximum |
|--------|--------|---------|
| Total Exports | <80 | 100 |
| Public Methods | <150 | 200 |
| Public Classes | <20 | 30 |
| Builder Methods | <40 | 50 |
| Complexity Score | <150 | 200 |

## JSON Declarative Format

For simple experiments, use JSON instead of code:

```json
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "metadata": {
    "title": "Simple Reaction Time",
    "author": "Researcher Name"
  },
  "timelines": [
    {
      "id": "main",
      "trials": [
        {
          "stimulus": {
            "type": "text",
            "content": "Press SPACE when you see the X",
            "duration": 2000
          }
        },
        {
          "stimulus": {
            "type": "text",
            "content": "X"
          },
          "response": {
            "type": "keyboard",
            "keys": ["space"],
            "timeout": 5000
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

### Loading JSON Experiments

```typescript
import { JSONExperimentLoader } from '@psyforge/forgescript';

// Load from JSON file
const experiment = JSONExperimentLoader.load('./experiments/stroop.json');

// Or from object
const experiment = JSONExperimentLoader.load({
  version: '1.0',
  metadata: { title: 'My Experiment' },
  timelines: [...]
});
```

### Converting to JSON

```typescript
import { JSONExperimentExporter } from '@psyforge/forgescript';

// Export existing experiment
const json = JSONExperimentExporter.export(experiment);
console.log(JSON.stringify(json, null, 2));
```

## Deprecation Process

### Marking APIs as Deprecated

```typescript
/**
 * @deprecated Since version 2.0. Use `stimulus.text()` instead.
 * @see {@link https://docs.psyforge.org/migration/v2}
 */
export function createTextStimulus(content: string) {
  DeprecationWarning.warn(
    'createTextStimulus',
    'stimulus.text()',
    '4.0'
  );
  return stimulus.text(content);
}
```

### Deprecation Timeline

1. **v2.0** - Mark as deprecated, add warnings
2. **v3.0** - Increase warning visibility
3. **v4.0** - Remove from TypeScript types
4. **v5.0** - Remove completely

### Migration Tools

```bash
# Check for deprecated API usage
npm run migrate:check

# Automatically migrate code
npm run migrate:auto --from 1.0 --to 2.0

# Interactive migration
npm run migrate:interactive
```

## Best Practices

### 1. Progressive Disclosure

```typescript
// Simple use case - minimal API
const exp = experiment('Stroop Task')
  .stimulus.text('RED', { color: 'blue' })
  .response.keyboard(['f', 'g', 'h'])
  .build();

// Advanced use case - opt-in complexity
const exp = experiment('Complex Task')
  .configure(advancedOptions)
  .stimulus.custom(MyCustomStimulus)
  .response.custom(MyCustomResponse)
  .analysis.power({ effectSize: 0.5 })
  .build();
```

### 2. Consistent Naming

```typescript
// Good: Consistent action verbs
timeline.add(stimulus);
timeline.remove(stimulus);
timeline.clear();

// Bad: Inconsistent verbs
timeline.add(stimulus);
timeline.delete(stimulus);
timeline.empty();
```

### 3. Composable APIs

```typescript
// Good: Methods can be chained naturally
experiment()
  .trial(stimulus1, response1)
  .trial(stimulus2, response2)
  .repeat(10)
  .randomize()
  .build();

// Bad: Order-dependent or non-chainable
experiment.setTrials([...]);
experiment.enableRandomization();
experiment.setRepetitions(10);
```

## Monitoring API Health

### Regular Reviews

1. **Monthly** - Check complexity metrics
2. **Quarterly** - Review deprecated APIs
3. **Yearly** - Major API design review

### Metrics Dashboard

Access the API health dashboard:

```bash
npm run api:report
```

This shows:
- Complexity trends over time
- Most used vs least used APIs
- Deprecation migration progress
- Community feedback summary

## Decision Framework

When adding new APIs, consider:

1. **Is it necessary?** Can existing APIs handle this?
2. **Is it discoverable?** Will users find it easily?
3. **Is it consistent?** Does it match existing patterns?
4. **Is it future-proof?** Will this make sense in 2 years?
5. **Is it composable?** Does it work well with other APIs?

## Examples

### Good API Addition

```typescript
// RFC-0023: Add timing precision helper
experiment.requirePrecision('2ms'); // Clear, necessary, consistent
```

### Bad API Addition

```typescript
// Too specific, not composable
experiment.createStroopTaskWithCustomColors(
  words, colors, options, callbacks, validators
);
```

## Enforcement

### In Pull Requests

- API complexity check runs automatically
- New exports require justification
- Breaking changes require RFC reference

### In CI/CD

```yaml
- name: Check API Complexity
  run: npm run api:check
  
- name: Fail on API bloat
  run: |
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
      echo "API complexity exceeds thresholds"
      exit 1
    fi
```

## Getting Help

- Read the [RFC Process](./src/api/governance/RFC.md)
- Check [Deprecation Policy](./src/api/governance/DeprecationPolicy.md)
- Run `npm run api:check` for current metrics
- Ask in #api-design channel on Discord

input.content:
name: Security Scan

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  schedule:
    # Run daily security scan
    - cron: '0 0 * * *'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  security-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    permissions:
      contents: read
      security-events: write
      issues: write
      pull-requests: write
    
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      
      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm run build
      
      - name: Run dependency audit
        id: npm-audit
        run: |
          npm audit --json > npm-audit.json || true
          echo "audit_complete=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      
      - name: Run ForgeScript security scan
        id: security-scan
        run: |
          cd packages/forgescript
          npm run security:scan -- \
            --output junit \
            --output-file ../../security-report.xml \
            --fail-on high \
            --verbose || echo "scan_failed=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      
      - name: Generate markdown report
        if: always()
        run: |
          cd packages/forgescript
          npm run security:scan -- \
            --output markdown \
            --output-file ../../security-report.md \
            --fail-on high || true
      
      - name: Upload security reports
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: security-reports
          path: |
            security-report.xml
            security-report.md
            npm-audit.json
      
      - name: Publish test results
        if: always()
        uses: EnricoMi/publish-unit-test-result-action@v2
        with:
          files: security-report.xml
          check_name: Security Scan Results
          comment_title: Security Scan Results
      
      - name: Comment PR with security summary
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request' && always()
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const report = fs.readFileSync('security-report.md', 'utf8');
            
            // Truncate if too long
            const maxLength = 65000;
            const truncated = report.length > maxLength 
              ? report.substring(0, maxLength) + '\n\n... (truncated)'
              : report;
            
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: truncated
            });
      
      - name: SARIF conversion for GitHub Security
        if: always()
        run: |
          # Convert our security scan to SARIF format
          # This is a simplified example - in production use proper SARIF generator
          cat > security-scan.sarif << 'EOF'
          {
            "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/oasis-tcs/sarif-spec/master/Schemata/sarif-schema-2.1.0.json",
            "version": "2.1.0",
            "runs": [{

name: Security Scan

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  schedule:
    # Run daily security scan
    - cron: '0 0 * * *'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  security-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    permissions:
      contents: read
      security-events: write
      issues: write
      pull-requests: write
    
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      
      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'npm'
      
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm run build
      
      - name: Run dependency audit
        id: npm-audit
        run: |
          npm audit --json > npm-audit.json || true
          echo "audit_complete=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      
      - name: Run ForgeScript security scan
        id: security-scan
        run: |
          cd packages/forgescript
          npm run security:scan -- \
            --output junit \
            --output-file ../../security-report.xml \
            --fail-on high \
            --verbose || echo "scan_failed=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      
      - name: Generate markdown report
        if: always()
        run: |
          cd packages/forgescript
          npm run security:scan -- \
            --output markdown \
            --output-file ../../security-report.md \
            --fail-on high || true
      
      - name: Upload security reports
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: security-reports
          path: |
            security-report.xml
            security-report.md
            npm-audit.json
      
      - name: Publish test results
        if: always()
        uses: EnricoMi/publish-unit-test-result-action@v2
        with:
          files: security-report.xml
          check_name: Security Scan Results
          comment_title: Security Scan Results
      
      - name: Comment PR with security summary

        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const report = fs.readFileSync('security-report.md', 'utf8');
            
            // Truncate if too long
            const maxLength = 65000;
            const truncated = report.length > maxLength 
              ? report.substring(0, maxLength) + '\n\n... (truncated)'
              : report;
            
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: truncated
            });

            }]
